icd_category: A04.7 — Enterocolitis due to Clostridium difficile
one_liner: A 68-year-old woman presents with 5 days of profuse watery diarrhea and crampy lower abdominal pain. She finished a 10-day course of clindamycin two weeks ago for a dental abscess.

stage2.hpi_extension: She is having up to 12 loose, foul-smelling, non-bloody stools per day. She reports diffuse crampy abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, anorexia, and mild nausea. She has been hospitalized once in the past year for community-acquired pneumonia treated with ceftriaxone. She lives alone, eats home-cooked meals, and has had no sick contacts. She is worried because she feels weaker each day.
stage2.social_history: Retired schoolteacher. Non-smoker, no alcohol. Recent dental procedure with 10-day course of clindamycin.
stage2.physical_exam: General: mildly ill, dry mucous membranes. Abdomen: mild diffuse tenderness, most prominent in the lower quadrants; hyperactive bowel sounds, no rebound or guarding, no palpable masses. Rectal exam: loose stool in the vault, no gross blood. Skin: reduced turgor. Cardiac/pulm: unremarkable. Neuro: nonfocal.

lab_results.result: Stool Clostridioides difficile PCR for toxin B gene (tcdB): POSITIVE. Stool C. difficile glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) antigen: POSITIVE. Toxin A/B enzyme immunoassay: POSITIVE. Routine stool culture and ova/parasites: no other pathogens identified.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A positive GDH antigen with confirmatory toxin EIA (or toxin gene PCR) in a symptomatic patient with recent antibiotic exposure confirms C. difficile infection. Asymptomatic colonization is common, so testing requires clinically significant diarrhea.

lab_results.result: WBC 18.5 with left shift (marked leukocytosis). Hgb 13.1. Platelets 265. Smear: reactive neutrophilia.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A WBC >15,000 is a marker of severe C. difficile infection per IDSA criteria and should prompt oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin rather than metronidazole.

lab_results.result: Na 133, K 3.2, Cl 102, HCO3 20, BUN 28, Cr 1.6 (baseline 0.8), glucose 104, albumin 3.1.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypokalemic metabolic acidosis from diarrheal losses with a rising creatinine also meets severe disease criteria (Cr >1.5x baseline).

stage5.final_clue: She is admitted, rehydrated, and started on oral vancomycin 125 mg every 6 hours. Her diarrhea and leukocytosis improve over the next 3 days. On discharge she is instructed to discontinue her proton pump inhibitor and avoid unnecessary antibiotics.

debrief.classic_presentation: Clostridioides difficile infection classically presents with profuse, non-bloody, foul-smelling watery diarrhea and crampy abdominal pain in a patient with recent antibiotic exposure (classic offenders: clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, broad-spectrum cephalosporins, carbapenems). Other risk factors include advanced age, hospitalization, nursing-home residence, and proton pump inhibitor use. Severe disease can progress to pseudomembranous colitis (yellow-white plaques on colonoscopy), toxic megacolon, and bowel perforation. Fever, leukocytosis >15,000, and rising creatinine mark severe disease.
debrief.pathophysiology: C. difficile is a gram-positive, spore-forming, obligate anaerobic rod. Antibiotics disrupt normal colonic flora, allowing ingested spores to germinate and proliferate. Pathogenic strains produce two exotoxins: Toxin A (enterotoxin, causes fluid secretion) and Toxin B (cytotoxin, glycosylates Rho GTPases and disrupts the actin cytoskeleton), which together injure colonocytes and form the fibrinopurulent pseudomembranes seen on endoscopy. The hypervirulent NAP1/BI/027 strain produces more toxin and causes more severe disease.
debrief.treatment_overview: Stop the offending antibiotic whenever possible. First-line therapy for an initial non-severe or severe episode is oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily or oral fidaxomicin for 10 days. Metronidazole is no longer preferred for adults except when vancomycin/fidaxomicin are unavailable. Fulminant disease (ileus, megacolon, hypotension) requires high-dose oral vancomycin with IV metronidazole and possible colectomy. For multiply recurrent infection, fecal microbiota transplantation has high cure rates. Contact isolation with soap-and-water hand washing is essential because alcohol-based hand sanitizer does not kill spores.
high_yield_pearls: Classic offenders = Clindamycin, Cephalosporins, Ciprofloxacin/fluoroquinolones ('the 3 C's').
Pseudomembranous colitis = yellow-white plaques on colonoscopy; toxin B glycosylates Rho GTPases.
Oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin are first-line; fecal microbiota transplant for multiply recurrent disease.
Spores are alcohol-resistant — use soap-and-water hand washing and contact precautions.
Test only symptomatic patients (>=3 unformed stools/24h); positive GDH antigen plus toxin EIA (or toxin gene PCR) is diagnostic.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Microbiology chapter, 'Clostridia — Clostridium difficile / Pseudomembranous colitis' section.
